fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need a small permissions helper library — something that wraps the raw OS permission result codes into something more usable. right now devs are scattering booleans like isDenied / neverAsk / shouldExplain all over the place and it's a mess, things keep getting conflated especially the 'denied forever' vs 'just denied for now' distinction. we want a typed model with clean predicates so you can just ask 'is this granted?' or 'does any permission in this set still need asking?' without writing loops every time.

also needs a request lifecycle thing — you should be able to attach listeners to a request and get notified when results come in. there's a specific flavor of listener that should fire exactly once and then clean itself up automatically (similar to how we handled the one-time auth callback in the login module, check that for reference). and there should be a builder to assemble a request — it needs to validate its inputs upfront and give back a clear reason when something's missing rather than blowing up weirdly at runtime. the validation order matters here, make sure you check the right thing first.

code organization matters — don't dump everything in one file but don't over-engineer it either. the JSON test harness is just for the adapter layer, keep the core domain totally separate from that. SOLID principles should be visible in the structure.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fun build(permissionsMode: String, permissions: List<String>?, provideHandler: Boolean): Either<BuildError, PermissionRequest>

trigger_keywords: how many top-level status categories are there
what are the valid permission status types
what values can the status type field take
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are exactly four mutually exclusive top-level status categories: 'granted', 'permanently_denied', 'should_show_rationale', and 'request_required'. The two denied sub-kinds ('permanently_denied' and 'should_show_rationale') both belong to a broader 'denied' category, so denied is not itself a top-level category but a grouping. For any single status, exactly one of {granted, denied, request_required} is true, and within denied, exactly one of {permanently_denied, should_show_rationale} is true.

trigger_keywords: what predicates does a single status expose
which boolean checks are available on a status
how many predicates does classify_status output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A single status must report exactly five independent boolean predicates: 'granted', 'denied' (true for any denied sub-kind), 'permanently_denied', 'should_show_rationale', and 'request_required'. The output format is the permission identifier first (permission=<id>), then one key=boolean line per predicate in that order.

trigger_keywords: what quantifiers does aggregation produce
what does aggregate_statuses output
how are collective predicates formatted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Aggregation over a list of statuses produces ten quantifier lines: for each of the five categories (granted, denied, permanently_denied, should_show_rationale, request_required), an 'all_*' flag followed by an 'any_*' flag. The output starts with 'count=<n>', then all five 'all_*' lines in order, then all five 'any_*' lines in the same category order. all_* is true only when every element belongs to that category; any_* is true when at least one element does.

trigger_keywords: how does the one-shot callback work
when does the one-shot listener detach itself
can the one-shot callback fire more than once
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A one-shot callback registers itself as exactly one listener on the request. When the very first result delivery occurs, the callback fires with that delivery's status list and then immediately removes itself from the listener registry. Any subsequent deliveries find zero listeners and the callback never fires again. The output must report: listeners_after_register=1, then for each delivery a 'delivery[i]_listeners_before' line, then callbacks_fired (always 1 after the first delivery), then the content of each fired callback as 'callback[i]=type:permission,type:permission' (or '<empty>'), then listeners_remaining=0.

trigger_keywords: what order does the builder validate inputs
which error does the builder return first
what happens if both permissions and handler are missing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The builder validates permissions first, then the handler provider. If permissions were never supplied (mode is 'unset'), the error is 'missing_permissions' regardless of whether a handler was provided. Only if permissions are present but no handler provider is attached does the builder return 'missing_runtime_handler'. This order is strict and must not be reversed.

trigger_keywords: what are the two ways to supply permissions to the builder
what is vararg mode vs list mode
are vararg and list permissions equivalent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The builder accepts permissions in two equivalent forms: 'vararg' (a first-plus-rest sequence, i.e., the first permission plus additional ones) and 'list' (a single list of permissions). Both forms are semantically identical and both count as 'permissions supplied'. The 'unset' mode means no permissions were provided at all. On success, the output is 'built=true' followed by 'request_is_permission_request=true'.

trigger_keywords: does the listener registry allow duplicate listeners
what happens if you add the same listener id twice
is the listener collection a set or a list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The listener registry uses set semantics, not multiset/list semantics. Adding the same listener id a second time has no effect — the listener is still present exactly once. A listener can be detached individually by id (remove) or all listeners can be removed at once (remove_all). After remove_all, a subsequent notify reaches zero listeners.

trigger_keywords: in what order are listener results reported
how is the feature5 output structured
how are per-listener received results formatted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the listener registry feature, the output structure is: for each notify operation in order, one 'notify[i]_recipients=<n>' line; then 'listeners=<final_count>'; then for each listener id in ascending lexicographic (alphabetical) id order, a 'received[id]_count=<n>' line followed by one 'received[id][j]=<content>' line per result received, in delivery order. Status content is rendered as 'type:permission' entries joined by commas, or '<empty>' for an empty result list.

trigger_keywords: how should the codebase be organized
should everything go in one file
what are the separate modules or components
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase must be split into distinct logical units matching the distinct responsibilities: (1) the status type model, (2) predicate/quantifier helpers, (3) the listener registry, (4) the one-shot callback, (5) the request builder/validator, and (6) the execution adapter (JSON stdin/stdout). The core domain must be completely decoupled from JSON parsing and stdin/stdout. Do not collapse everything into one file, but do not create unnecessary layers either.

trigger_keywords: what is the one-time auth callback reference
what login module pattern should I follow
what does the login module compatibility logic refer to
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The reference to 'the one-time auth callback in the login module' in the ticket is an internal analogy pointing to the one-shot callback pattern: a listener that automatically removes itself from the registry after firing exactly once. Concretely this means: register the callback as a normal listener, but inside the callback's onResult handler, call remove(self) on the registry before or after invoking the user's callback function, ensuring it cannot fire a second time and cannot leak.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'classify_status', 'aggregate_statuses', 'one_shot_result', 'build_request', 'listener_registry'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the one-time auth callback in the login module, check that for reference
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The OneShotResultCallback pattern described in Feature 3: a listener that calls remove(self) on the request's listener registry immediately after its first invocation, so it cannot fire again and the listener count drops to zero

context_pointers.vague_reference: the validation order matters here, make sure you check the right thing first
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The builder must validate permissions before the handler provider: missing_permissions is returned if no permissions were supplied regardless of handler state; missing_runtime_handler is only returned when permissions are present but handler is absent

context_pointers.vague_reference: the JSON test harness is just for the adapter layer
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter (the component reading JSON from stdin and writing line-oriented key=value text to stdout) must be the only component that touches JSON parsing; all domain logic (status model, predicates, registry, builder) must be in separate files with no JSON dependency

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now. Please check the existing codebase structure and test cases for guidance, and make sure whatever you implement keeps the core domain logic fully separated from the I/O adapter layer. When in doubt, prioritize making the typed status model and builder validation behavior match what the test harness expects.

feature1_status_classification.json.description: Classify a single permission status into the full set of category predicates. The input is a status object with a category type and the permission identifier it relates to; the output reports, for that one status, the boolean result of each independent predicate: whether it counts as granted, as denied (any denied sub-kind), as permanently denied, as needing a rationale, and as still requiring a runtime request. Exactly one of granted / denied / request-required is true for any status, and the two denied sub-kinds (permanently denied vs. should-show-rationale) are mutually exclusive within the denied category.

cases.expected_output:
permission=android.permission.SEND_SMS
granted=true
denied=false
permanently_denied=false
should_show_rationale=false
request_required=false

permission=android.permission.SEND_SMS
granted=false
denied=true
permanently_denied=false
should_show_rationale=true
request_required=false

feature2_status_aggregation.json.description: Aggregate a list of permission statuses into collective predicates. The input is an ordered list of status objects (each with a category type and permission identifier). The output reports the size of the list, then for every category a pair of quantifiers: an `all_*` flag (true only when every element of the list belongs to that category) and an `any_*` flag (true when at least one element belongs to it). The categories covered are: granted, denied (any denied sub-kind), permanently denied, should-show-rationale, and request-required. An empty-category list yields false for the corresponding flags; mixed lists let `any_*` be true while `all_*` is false.

cases.expected_output:
count=2
all_granted=true
all_denied=false
all_permanently_denied=false
all_should_show_rationale=false
all_request_required=false
any_granted=true
any_denied=false
any_permanently_denied=false
any_should_show_rationale=false
any_request_required=false

count=2
all_granted=false
all_denied=false
all_permanently_denied=false
all_should_show_rationale=false
all_request_required=false
any_granted=true
any_denied=true
any_permanently_denied=true
any_should_show_rationale=false
any_request_required=false

feature3_one_shot_callback.json.description: One-shot result callback on a permission request. A callback is registered on a request to receive a single result delivery; after the first delivery fires the callback, the callback auto-detaches so subsequent deliveries do not invoke it again. The input lists the sequence of result deliveries the request emits (each delivery is a list of permission statuses). The output reports the number of registered listeners right after registration, the number of listeners present immediately before each delivery, how many times the callback fired, the contents of each fired callback, and the number of listeners remaining at the end (which must be zero after the first delivery because the one-shot callback removes itself).

feature4_request_builder.json.description: Build a permission request through the configuration builder and validate required configuration. The builder needs two things before it can produce a request: the set of permissions to request and a runtime handler provider. The input declares how permissions are supplied (`unset` = not supplied, `vararg` = supplied as a first-plus-rest sequence, `list` = supplied as a single list) and whether a handler provider is attached. On a valid configuration the output confirms the request was built and that it conforms to the request abstraction. On an invalid configuration the output is a normalized error category: `missing_permissions` when no permissions were supplied, or `missing_runtime_handler` when permissions were supplied but no handler provider was attached. Permissions are validated before the handler.

feature5_listener_registry.json.description: Manage the listener registry of a permission request and notify all currently attached listeners. A request keeps a set of listeners; each `notify` delivers a result to exactly the listeners attached at that moment. Listeners are identified by a stable id; adding the same id twice has no extra effect (a set, not a multiset); a listener can be detached individually, or every listener can be detached at once. The input is an ordered list of operations: attach a listener (`add`), detach one (`remove`), detach all (`remove_all`), or emit a result to current listeners (`notify`). The output reports, for each notify, how many listeners received it; then the final listener count; then, per listener id, how many results it received and the content of each received result. A detached listener stops receiving results from that point on, and after detaching all, a subsequent notify reaches nobody.

cases.expected_output:
notify[0]_recipients=2
notify[1]_recipients=1
listeners=1
received[A]_count=1
received[A][0]=granted:p
received[B]_count=2
received[B][0]=granted:p
received[B][1]=granted:p
